speaker: interviewer
text: This is June 23, 1995. The survivor is Joseph Aleksander. The interviewer is Mary Rothschild. We are in Los Angeles, California, United States of America. It will be conducted in English. It is June 23, 1995. I am Mary Rothschild interviewing Joseph Aleksander for the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is June 23, 1995. This is tape number 1 with Joseph Aleksander. Please give us your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Joseph Aleksander, J-O-S-E-P-H A-L-E-K-S-A-N-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is August the 3rd, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Warsaw, Poland. At that time, my name was Joseph with an F at the end. I also had a middle name Leib, L-E-I-B. And my nickname was Lolek. My friends used to call me Lolek-- and my relatives, and so forth. But after the war, I dropped the word 'Lolek,' you know. I wasn't too crazy about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you remember from your childhood, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I remember from my childhood is that it-- you know, times were not that great most of the time. It was during the Depression. And things were very hard for most everybody. And I remember wishing for many things that, that I couldn't have, even as a child, for a nice rocking horse and, and nice toys that I didn't have, and then later and I-- later on for nice clothing, which I couldn't have. Most of the time I wore hand-me-downs from my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So times were quite tough. So anybody that said that Jews were all rich, is, is-- it's not true, you know. What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-- my brother's name was Joel, J-O-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joel is one year and two months older than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were pretty close, not, not really that close. My, my brother had his own friends. And I had my own friends, I guess, because of the age difference, slight as it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the home you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not a very large apartment. We lived on the second floor. And it was one very large room, basically, and a kitchen and a bathroom. And the room was kind of divided by-- with moving walls, so-- into separate quarters for my parents and for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Abram or Abraham. And my mother's name was Pola-- in Jewish, was [? Pesa. ?] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke mostly Polish at home. My parents spoke mostly Yiddish to themselves but Polish to us, for some reason, so that I didn't really learn to speak very well Yiddish until later on. Actually, I really learned to speak Yiddish well once I was in a camp-- in the first camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a buyer in the textile field. And he used to get orders from out of town-- from many small towns. And he-- and he used to fill the orders from the wholesalers in Warsaw and ship the-- they used to make packages and ship them to these-- to the merchants in the small towns all over-- all over Poland. So that was basically what he was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well my mother, as far as I remember in the beginning while we were growing up, was a housewife. But then later on, when things got very tough, you know, like in the late 1930s, she went to work for a company that was making corsets, you know, for women, foundations-- foundation garments. And then, after a while, she learned how to do it. And, and she started manufacturing it herself. And then at the end, she had, like, two girls sewing. She-- they had about three sewing machines. They used to sew those corsets. And she used to go and-- to different well-to-do houses, you know, the women. And she used to fit them in-- fit them for it. Because over there, it was mostly custom-made. It was not like here that you go to the store, and you get it ready-made. Over there was-- it's just like a suit. Over there, you had it made. So the same thing was with corsets. So that's-- so after-- once she started doing that, we were-- we were doing a lot better financially. So she was a big help, actually. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father used to go to the synagogue every Friday night and Saturday. And he insisted that we go, too, of course, and on all the High Holidays. And he used to put out the [NON-ENGLISH] every day. So I guess that he was religious. But he didn't force the religion onto us. And somehow or other, I never really became religious. I, I mean, we used to observe Saturday-- you know Friday night and Saturday. And, and we used to go to the temple on all the holidays. But, of course, as kids, we couldn't wait to get outside and play ball. You know, we couldn't wait for the time where you-- where the kids could get out, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday night, I remember the candles and the, the fish-- gefilte fish was the-- chicken soup, and all the good stuff. And then my mother used to make cholent every week, a, a big pot, pot of cholent. And my brother and I used to take it to the bakery Friday. And then Saturday morning or at noon, we used to go and pick up the cholent. And I remember the smell was always so good of it, you know. We used to wrap up the pot with paper. And the paper was all brown and crispy after the cholent was baked. This is what I mostly remember about Friday night and Saturday, you know, pleasant memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in the Jewish quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the neighborhood that we lived was mostly Jewish, but it was, was mixed. There were some Gentiles. But I would say probably about 75%, 80% Jewish in that area. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were all way, you know. There were secular people. And there were religious people, you know. We were probably considered secular, you know. There were some people that, you know, they were Hasidim, you know, with the, you know, long beards and-- but it was mostly secular. And we used to play soccer, you know, which is-- we used to call it football-- a lot on the fields. The neighborhood that we lived, it was not far from a big, empty space. It used to be called [NON-ENGLISH] in, in Warsaw. And we used to spend a lot of time there playing. Also there were a lot of parks. Park Traugutta, you know, was over there. So as I remember from my childhood, a lot of games, and playing, and chases, and all this kind of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your school? Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to school-- for a while I went to a Jewish school, which was [NON-ENGLISH] school, you know, the grade school. But I guess they couldn't afford it. So they sent me to a-- my parents send me to a regular grade school. And it was mostly Christian. There were only about three or four Jews in the class, in that school. And that's-- and that wasn't very pleasant because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: --we used to get beat up all the time by the Polacks. They could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get beat up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, many times. After a while, we kind of learned to be tolerated. But every once in a while when a new boy would come in, he had to, you know, start beating up Jewish kids, you know. We were mostly-- the Polack-- Polish kids were bigger than we were because they, they were older, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they saying to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mostly they would say, [SPEAKING POLISH], which means you lousy Jew, you know. And, and as a matter of fact, it comes to my mind now that they used to teach catechism there. And the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] priest would come to teach catechism in school. And he would say, [SPEAKING POLISH], you know. It means the lousy Jews, get out of here. That was from the priest himself, yeah. So those were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started school when I was six in the-- in the-- in the grade school, you know. That, that, that much I remember. And, and then I-- after, after this school was over, seven grades of this school, I went into trade high school, which was a lot different because we were all Jewish there. Because it was supported by Jewish trade organizations, you know. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when these anti-Semitic incidents happened in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, starting about 6 till about-- till about 13 or so, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- what were you feeling? What were you thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were, were feelings of fear, you know, because you were-- many times, my father had to come and pick me up from school because you were afraid that they will gang up and beat you up, you know. And I also remember on vacations was the same, same thing. You know, if you-- if you were someplace where there was a majority of the Polacks, you know, you were always afraid of getting beat up. And also on the streets of Warsaw, you had to be careful where you went. Because if you went to a Polish neighborhood, it was dangerous, you know. My brother was very tough, you know. He, he used to defend me. He was a real tough kid, you know. He could fight two, three of them at one time. He was very strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go on vacation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they usually-- we used to go, like, to camps, you know like a summer camp, you know, to Kazimierz and other neighborhoods, you know. And that was pleasant. It was nice, you know. Because it was-- the reason it was nice, because we were all Jewish. There was nobody to beat us up. [LAUGHS] Unless you strayed out of the camp someplace, then you had to be careful, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my grandfather was very religious. I still have his picture. He had a long white beard and payos, you know. And he, he made his living as a teamster. He was driving a horse-- you know, a horse and wagon, you know, wagon with horses. That's how he made his living, you know. But he was also active in the shul and synagogue. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Sachne Schulman-- last name was Schulman. He was a very nice man. He was a very nice man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we saw him quite often, yeah. Sometimes I used to go over there and stay with him, you know. He was a very nice man. And I have memories of him bringing us toys sometimes, you know, little shovels, you know, to play in the sand and all when we were young. He was a nice man. He actually went to Israel in the-- before the war in the '30s, and-- while I was still-- while I was still young. And I understand he had to work very hard in Israel for a man his age, you know. And that's what hastened his death. You know, he worked as a pioneer there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother lived on for a long time, for a-- she died, I think, in the 1960s or something like that, quite at an advanced age. She also died in Israel, yes, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your paternal grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew them. I never knew anybody from my father's side. I understand that he had family in this country, somebody. But I, I never met anybody from his side of the family, mostly-- only from my mother's side of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father strict?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was a doll. He was very soft-- very soft-hearted. He could never go out by a beggar without giving him some money-- a very good-natured, soft-hearted person. Both of them were, actually. And my mother always had people at home that were worst off than we were, you know. And they would stay for a night or many nights. And she used to feed them-- a very charitable person, yeah. And I remember many times when we didn't have it, other relatives took care of us, you know. People would help each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school when you became 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started going-- I went to-- I think, at the age of 14, I went to this school called [POLISH], in Polish. It was like a trade high school. It was-- we had half, half a day of theory and a half a day of practice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- on a, a low level of machinist work, you know, mostly working by hand with files and hammers and drills and, and then, at the end, going to more an advanced machinist work, running all different-- you know, like lathes and mills and shapers, and machines of this kind, you know-- at the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really didn't like it that much, to tell you the truth. But my father felt that I should have a trade. He says-- he used to say a man-- a [NON-ENGLISH] in Jewish means a tradesman. A [NON-ENGLISH] will always make a living, he said. So that's why-- that was his influence on that. In Europe, you didn't argue with your parents. You did mostly what your parents asked you-- told you to do. My brother was sent to a-- to a business school. So he developed in that direction, in businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your dreams? What did you want to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I want-- as a young person? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't really remember, you know. I, I believe just-- for a better life, I suppose. I used to want-- my, my greatest love was sports. I used to belong to a club called Maccabi. And we used to do gymnastics and group gymnastics. And we used to-- as a matter of fact, once a year, they had, like, a, a performance in the circus. And we used to do our gymnastics there. And I used to do track and field. And I, I enjoyed it very much. That was probably my biggest love, is my-- you know, belonging to Maccabi and being amongst other children my own age-- or young men and women my own age, and doing gymnastics, and doing track and field, and swimming. I think that, that was probably my brightest side of my life in Europe at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because we were-- we didn't even have a radio, you know, in those days, no less TVs and everything like they have now. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I used to read a lot. I always loved to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what did you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my favorite books were Sir Arthur Conan Doyle, Zane Grey, and basically, everything about Lawrence of Arabia, and all this-- anything that had something to, to do with adventures and, and with-- the Wild West stories, and all kinds of stories like that. And then-- I, I loved to read. I used to read almost a book a day. Because basically, the-- you know, that was the entertainment in those days. Because we didn't have radio and TV and tapes like the children have now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any close friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had very many close friends. And unfortunately, I don't know any that survived. We had a good, close circle of friends. And-- but as the ghetto closed up, you kind of lost sight of each other. And after a while, there was nobody left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: See, what happened to me is that I got sick in the-- as they, when they started. Should I start with the ghetto already? Are you ready for that? Well, before we go there, do you remember any incidents of anti-Semitism before the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, many, many incidents-- many incidents. Like, one, one year, I was in a-- my parents used to send me out to a, a-- in summertime-- we had some relatives that lived in small towns, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I forget-- near the-- near a river-- Bug, you know, near the river Bug. And I was once surrounded by a bunch of Polish kids. And they had-- they, they beat me up with the oars, with the-- you know, they had, like, a boat. And they, they used the oars to beat me up. And they were not happy until I started crying. And then they left me alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 13. Actually, the reason I was there-- because I had my bar mitzvah over there. Brzesc nad Bugiem was the name of the, the town. Because my parents couldn't afford to give me a bar mitzvah at home, so they sent me out there. It was a lot cheaper. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's one of the incidents. And there were many incidents in the city, you know, that-- being attacked by Polish kids. And the names, you know, that they used to call us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me, I'll have a drink of water.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What were the first signs of trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you that the, the first signs of trouble was-- were that the anti-Semitism in Poland really grew to a, a very big degree. And, as a matter of fact, there was a boycott of anything that's Jewish. And the, the Cardinal Wyszynski said to, to boycott the Jews is OK, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the business really got bad. And, and that attacks of anti-Semitism were very bad. So that actually when the Germans started attacking, some Jews thought maybe it'll be for the better. It couldn't be much for the worst. Little did we know-- What happened to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? At that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- when, when the Germans came in? Well, my, my mother was still doing her trade for a while. My father couldn't do anything anymore. It was-- his business kind of died down completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, thanks God, my mother was doing still her business. She used to go to the Polish ladies, who had the-- you know, who were well off. And, and she used to, you know, work for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I-- after I graduated from school, I started working in a-- in a tool-and-die shop. I was apprenticed or, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, doing some of the work. And, and then the ghetto started. They started closing up the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1939, I was 16. See, the, the school was three year-- three-year school. So I actually graduated at 16. I was the youngest. Most kids were 17 or 18. And then, as the ghetto closed up, I went to work for a factory that used to make carbide lamps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I used-- [LAUGHS] to, to, to supplement the income, I used to sell lamps to, to friends, you know. I used to get them very cheap in the factory and used to sell them to people that we know. Because we still had some friends. We still used to get together. In the beginning, the ghetto wasn't as terrible as it was later on. We still had friends. And we used to get together. And we had our dances. We played ping pong and volleyball. Because it wasn't as tightly compacted as it-- as it became later.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- describe the living conditions. Were you able to stay in your own apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a-- for a while we were, until they really-- but then they started closing off the ghetto, in other words, making it smaller constantly. So then when they took away the area-- see, we lived right on the border of the ghetto. The street's name was [POLISH], you know. And it was right, like, on the edge of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were one of the first that were taken off. Then we went live with some relatives, some distant relatives. And, and that was harder. And I became very sick. I had a fistula on the edge of my rectum.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to be sick in the ghetto wasn't good. So I had to soak and doing this. And there was no real medical care there. And that was very tough. And then they started the raids, you know, to-- they used to pick up people on the street and send them to Treblinka. And one of those was my father. They picked him up quite early. I think it was in-- around 1940, '41, so-- '40, yes. So he was one of the first that they picked up. And as a matter of fact, unfortunately, the Jewish police used to help in gathering the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out they picked up your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody told us that he was in a group of people that they circled around, you know, and shoved him into a-- I think it was a streetcar or something. And they took him away. And we never saw him again. Otherwise, he wouldn't be away. But we never saw him again. So that was the first thing that I lost-- was my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 17 at that time, so-- that time about 17. That was a tragedy. And then-- and then they started to tighten the ghetto more and more. And I was still sick. I couldn't work. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at one time, we even went out of the ghetto to work for Polish farmers-- at the beginning of the ghetto. I'm going back now a little bit, when they first closed the ghetto. You could still get out. So we went out to work for Polish farmers. And I worked for a few months. But then we saw that there was a war coming out between Germany and Russia. As I was standing-- the, the place that I was working was the Polish Ukraine. And there was-- you could see the German Army massing and coming into Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we thought we'd better come and go back home. So we went back to the ghetto. It was-- like I say, in those days, we didn't know that it's going to be so terrible. So we went back to the ghetto. And, and then we-- I had-- I had-- we had relatives that we stayed with. And they owned, like, a wood-- a small-sized wood mill where they made wood molding and boards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on that-- on, on, on the-- on the-- on that lot of that mill, the, the Germans came to recruit people for work in a labor camp. I found out about it because one of the girls that we stayed with, one of the young women, she, she told us about it. And I couldn't go there because I couldn't even walk, you know. My fistula was really bothering me. So she put me in one of those rickshaws, you know. They were like a bicycle-driven rickshaw. And she, she wheeled me over on that-- on that lot. And I had my diploma from school that I graduated as a machinist journeyman. So when the Germans were selecting the people to go and work in that labor camp, I presented my diploma. And they took me, which was a big break to get out of the ghetto. And, and this way, I saved myself for the time being.
